topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Albright stared at the package on his worn oak desk, a crumpled piece of wrapping paper mocking his composure. It was addressed in looping, familiar script—a script he hadn't seen in nearly a decade. Inside, nestled in tissue paper, was a vintage, leather-bound copy of a rare poetry collection he’d been searching for, along with a handwritten note. Albright picked up the note, his jaw tightening until the muscles in his neck corded. *How dare he? After all these years, this intrusion?* He reread the elegant, almost mocking prose. A slow, deep breath escaped him, shaky and uneven. He pushed the collection away, the sound of the paper rustling sounding deafening in the quiet office. He needed to burn it. He needed to erase the memory of that student’s face from his mind.

[story 2]

“Seriously?” Dr. Evans muttered, his voice tight enough to snap. The gift sat on the edge of his mahogany desk: a meticulously crafted, antique compass, the brass gleaming under the fluorescent lights. It was from Mark, a student from his advanced physics course who’d drifted away after graduation. Evans slammed his palm flat on the wood, the resulting thud echoing sharply in the empty faculty lounge. His knuckles were white where he gripped the edge of the desk. He paced three steps toward the window, his gaze fixed on the indifferent city traffic below. Every instinct screamed at him to snatch the item, to destroy the evidence of this unwelcome connection. He felt a hot flush rising up his chest, a furious, buzzing energy that made his hands tremble uncontrollably.

[story 3]

I picked up the box with two fingers, my grip surprisingly firm. It was a collection of obscure vinyl records, something I hadn't thought about in years. The handwriting on the card was unmistakable. My breath hitched; the air in the small classroom suddenly felt too thick to draw in. I felt a sudden, sharp tightening behind my eyes, a pressure building behind my temples. *This is unnecessary. This is inappropriate.* I turned the gift over in my hands, the cardboard cool against my skin. I walked to the filing cabinet, grabbed a heavy textbook, and brought it down hard onto the desk, the sound a deliberate, forceful punctuation mark to my rising internal storm.
